summary:
Elective caesarean sections are associated with an increased incidence of transient tachypnea of the newborn due to hormonal differences compared to vaginal births; approximately 4-5% of term infants experience elective c-sections each year, making it a common situation where TTN occurs. Key factors include reduced exposure to beneficial maternal hormones post-labor onset leading to delayed resolution of pulmonary edema/fluid accumulation characteristic of TTN. To summarize concisely:

Transient tachypnea often affects full-term babies delivered via elective Cesarean because they miss hormonally-induced benefits aiding normal breathing after birth. This practice increases their likelihood by about 1-in-20 cases among all term livebirths globally.
